result.content: FINAL_ANSWER: To solve the Polyomino Mining problem with minimal probing cost, implement a Bayesian-inspired adaptive sampling strategy combined with a geometric pattern-matching algorithm. 1. Probabilistic Mapping: Maintain a probability map P(c) for every cell c in the grid, representing the likelihood that c belongs to an oil-containing polyomino. Initially, P(c) is determined by the density and possible placements of the known polyomino shapes. 2. Information-Theoretic Probing (Active Learning): Instead of random drilling, select probe locations x that maximize the Expected Information Gain (EIG). Since queries are noisy aggregates, use a Bayesian update rule: P(c | query) proportional to P(query | c) * P(c). The objective function for selecting the next probe is to minimize the entropy of the global probability distribution. 3. Geometric Constraint Satisfaction: Use the known polyomino shapes as rigid templates. When a probe returns a signal, use a RANSAC-style or template-matching approach to filter possible polyomino placements. This significantly prunes the search space compared to cell-by-cell analysis. 4. Drilling vs. Aggregate Strategy: - Use Aggregate Queries early in the process to identify hot zones (high-density areas) across large regions. - Use Drilling (point queries) only when the uncertainty in a specific high-probability cluster exceeds a threshold, or to confirm the exact boundary of a detected polyomino. 5. Final Guessing: Once the cost budget is nearly exhausted, perform a Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation to select the set of cells that maximizes the likelihood of containing the polyominoes given all collected noisy data. JUSTIFICATION: This approach minimizes cost by prioritizing high-entropy regions through aggregate queries and uses the geometric rigidity of polyominoes to compensate for query noise, preventing redundant drilling.
